items.title: First case of deadly H5 bird flu confirmed in Australia

children.content: The virus, found in a migratory bird in Esperance, raises concerns for the poultry industry, despite Australia having spent years preparing for such a biosecurity breach.

articles.title: Why Australia's first H5 bird flu case is 'deeply concerning'

children.content: Over recent years avian influenza has made its way across the globe. Now Australia has its first case. Here's what we know.

items.title: Melbourne woman suffers acute liver failure after using knock-off drug

children.content: Megan Hancocks bought a drug labelled as the peptide compound retatrutide in cash from a beautician. Within a week of her second dose, she was in acute liver failure.

image.altText: A woman lies in a hospital bed with her eyes closed.

items.title: Tectonic plates are shifting as Trump's diabolical Iran deal plays out

children.content: Just as Netanyahu's claims to be able to influence Trump have now become a burden to him, Trump's limited capacity to influence Netanyahu spells just as much trouble for peace in the Middle East.

image.altText: Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu

items.title: Twin brothers born deaf hear their mum's voice for first time after epic surgery

children.content: An Australian-first eight hour surgical feat had one main purpose — for Artie and Jack to hear their mum's voice at the same time.

gallery.altText: Twin boys sitting in high chair smiling.

thumbnail.altText: Twin boys sitting in high chair smiling.

items.title: Socceroos receive World Cup wake-up call and have a week to find answers

children.content: Coming into the game on a high, the Socceroos copped a reality check in the first half against the USA. How they respond over the next week will define their 2026 World Cup campaign.

image.altText: Harry Souttar looks confronts Folarin Balogun as other players intervene

items.title: Socceroos live events marred by flares, alleged sexual assault

children.content: Thousands of people flocked to sites across Australia to show their support as the Socceroos fell short against World Cup host USA.

image.altText: A crowd of people.

items.title: Socceroos quick hits: Selection stunner leaves stars on bench, coach's VAR frustration

children.content: A Cameron Burgess own goal and VAR drama cost Australia against the US, before cramp proved a problem for the match referee. Here are the five quick hits from the Socceroos vs USA.

articles.title: Brazil turn on the style at World Cup against Haiti

children.content: Matheus Cunha scores scores twice for Brazil against Haiti, while Morocco defeats Scotland 1-0 thanks to the fastest goal at this World Cup so far. Follow live.

image.altText: Matheus Cunha holds his arms out

articles.title: 'He got that wrong': Schwarzer says selection mistakes cost Socceroos

children.content: The Socceroos will rue a missed opportunity after a dire first half against the USA, with Tony Popovic's selections at least partly to blame, says Mark Schwarzer.

image.altText: Tony Popovic looks stern

articles.title: How blind faith in the internet helped a man fulfill a World Cup promise

children.content: The general advice is if it seems too good to be true, then it probably is. For Ash Jurberg, a punt on an anonymous message on LinkedIn sent him to the World Cup and fulfilled a 16-year-old promise to his sons.

image.altText: Three men in soccer jersey's pose for a photo

items.title: 'Everything's just so wrong': Inside the country town that turned to One Nation

children.content: In just a few short weeks, a small country town became One Nation heartland. It is a blueprint for how the party can turn Australia orange.

image.altText: Older man wearing blue jumper and orange One nation hat stands with arms outstretched in front of service station

items.title: What does it mean for a nation to be a 'monoculture'?

children.content: Pauline Hanson has called for Australia to reject multiculturalism and be "monocultural". So what does it involve and are there any nations overseas that show what it might look like in Australia?

image.altText: People walk across a zebra crossing in a city with billboards in Japanese in the background.

items.title: As Trump praises 'very smart' leaders in Iran deal, Qalibaf is on the rise

children.content: When Iran and the United States inked a deal on the war this week, one unlikely figure on the Iranian side emerged from the shadows of the negotiations and into the open.

image.altText: ranian Supreme leader Ali Khamenei (C) with Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf (L) and Judiciary Chief Mohsen Ejeie (R)

items.title: If you didn't do it at 40, do it at 60

children.content: For some women, the act of asking, "what might still be possible" — of refusing to accept that the most meaningful chapters are already behind them — might be its own kind of resistance.

image.altText: Tilda Swinton poses on a red carpet wearing a red long sleeve dress and gold choker

items.title: The immersive Indigenous language class where English is left at the door

children.content: After learning from Native American Yuchi families, Pertame elders and young mothers in Alice Springs are raising babies in a total immersion Language Nest and hope to extend the model into primary school.

items.title: Sixteen years after Josh was murdered, will the case ever be solved?

children.content: The grieving mother of Josh Warneke, who was found dead on the side of a road in WA's remote Kimberly, has faced a long battle for answers. But how likely is it the 21-year-old's killer will be brought to justice?

image.altText: Image of a photograph of Josh Warneke placed in front of a memorial in Broome, Western Australia.

items.title: Why these retirees won't give up the 3:30am starts and 14-hour shifts

children.content: When the alarm goes off at 3:30am in this freezing outback pit-stop, it's music to the ears of the volunteer bakers who happily spend their winters in a bakery like no other.

items.title: So much for the promise that Tasmanians deserve better from politicians

children.content: After last year's snap election, Tasmania's premier declared voters were "sick and tired of the political games". After this week, it is clear that the politicians just cannot help themselves.

items.title: Why is your phone going off? Don't be alarmed, it's a warning system test

children.content: This week, phones in some regional towns have received a warning text alert and emitted an alarm as part of national warning system testing. Next month, your phone could receive it too.

items.title: Meloni hits back at Trump's claim she 'begged' him for photo

items.title: Gold Coast council reconsiders return of ocean pools

children.content: Its been decades since ocean pools had a presence along the Gold Coast shoreline. Now, council is considering whether they could make a comeback after strong community interest.
